mnemonics.title: CH Theorem Cheer
mnemonics.content: C-H Theorem: Put Matrix IN - Zero comes OUT! (Substitute matrix A into its characteristic polynomial p(λ), get p(A)=0)

mnemonics.title: Minimal Polynomial Finder
mnemonics.content: Minimal Polynomial = Smallest degree polynomial that kills A. Think: MINIMAL = MIN + I + MAL (Minimum Index at Largest Jordan block)

mnemonics.title: Trace & Determinant
mnemonics.content: TRACE = sum of eigenvalues (sum of diagonals). DETERMINANT = product of eigenvalues. Remember: TrAce Sums, DetAils Multiply!

mnemonics.title: Power Reduction
mnemonics.content: For 2×2: A² = tr(A)A - det(A)I. This is your magic wand to reduce any higher power!

acronyms.usage: Every square matrix satisfies its characteristic equation: p(A)=0
Used to find eigenvalues of matrix A

acronyms.expansion: Minimal Polynomial - monic polynomial of least degree that annihilates matrix
acronyms.usage: m(A) = 0, and m(x) divides every annihilating polynomial of A

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 2×2 matrix. Mark trace on main diagonal (top-left to bottom-right). Mark determinant in the corner. p(λ) = λ² - (trace)λ + (det). For 3×3, add minor determinants.
Write p(λ) = λⁿ + aₙ₋₁λⁿ⁻¹ + ... + a₀. Replace λ with A: p(A) = Aⁿ + aₙ₋₁Aⁿ⁻¹ + ... + a₀I = 0. It's like a magic trick - substitute and get zero!
Think of characteristic polynomial as a puzzle. Eigenvalues are the pieces (roots) that make det(A - λI) = 0. Each root λᵢ means A×vᵢ = λᵢ×vᵢ for some direction vᵢ.
Think of Jordan blocks as stairs. Largest stair height = degree of minimal polynomial for that eigenvalue. Stack all eigenvalues to get full minimal polynomial.

analogies.analogy: Just like substituting x=3 into x²-5x+6 gives 9-15+6=0, substituting matrix A into characteristic polynomial gives the zero matrix! The matrix follows the same rules as scalars.
If characteristic polynomial is a big hammer that crushes A to zero, minimal polynomial is the smallest possible tool that still does the job - no extra power wasted!
Eigenvalues are like natural frequencies of a system. They tell us how the matrix stretches or shrinks in certain special directions (eigenvectors). Similar to guitar string frequencies.
e^x = 1 + x + x²/2! + x³/3! + ... is how we extend exponentials to numbers. e^A does the same for matrices - it's the continuous version of matrix powers, used in solving differential equations.
